questions: Within the history and legal debates surrounding fanfiction, two authors are frequently cited for opposite reasons: one transformed a fan-written story based on a popular vampire romance series into a commercially published bestselling trilogy by substantially altering characters and setting (a practice often called “filing off the serial numbers”), while the other became well known for explicitly opposing and restricting fanfiction based on her vampire novels. Identify both authors and, for each, explain (a) the original source material involved, (b) the nature of their connection to fanfiction (adaptation into commercial fiction vs. prohibition), and (c) how their stance or actions influenced broader conversations about copyright and fair use in fan communities.

In a comprehensive online guide about writing and publishing fanfiction, several platforms are recommended for sharing stories. One of these platforms is described as allowing authors to publish on their own branded subdomain or fully custom domain, keep 0% of direct reader payments (aside from third‑party payment processing fees), choose between full-book pricing, chapter bundles, or subscriptions, serialize updates with automatic reader notifications, export mailing lists, and even participate in a platform governance system shaped by user delegates. 

Identify this platform and provide a detailed overview of: (1) how its payment structure works, including when any additional platform fees may apply; (2) the different monetization models authors can choose from; (3) what happens if an author decides to leave the platform; and (4) how it supports reader discovery. 

Then, drawing from the same broader ecosystem of resources, describe how the associated online public-domain book library is organized (including how titles are sorted and paginated) and name the major external projects credited with supplying many of its ebook files.

Within online fanfiction communities, one widely shared Harry Potter alternate-universe story reimagines the aftermath of the night Harry is left on Privet Drive: instead of being raised by his aunt and uncle, he is taken by police and adopted by a loving family, while Sirius Black—able to transform into a large black dog—eventually lives with him and later reveals his identity as Harry’s godfather. Identify this specific fan-created work and provide the following publicly verifiable details: the date it was originally posted, the online platform where it first appeared, the nickname used for Sirius in his canine form, and at least two ways the author organized or distributed the story (for example, tagging conventions, masterposts, or alternate hosting).

Identify the 2022 nonfiction article written by Cat Webling that provides an overview of fanfiction, tracing its literary roots from a medieval Italian poet’s religious epic through a 1913 Austen-inspired novel, to the rise of Sherlock Holmes pastiches and Star Trek fanzines such as a 1967 publication created by Gene Roddenberry and D.C. Fontana. Based on that article, provide: (1) its exact publication date, (2) the name of the medieval work cited as an early example of fanfiction, (3) the title and author of the 1913 Austen-related novel discussed, (4) the name of the 1967 Star Trek fanzine mentioned, and (5) the four factors used in U.S. copyright law to evaluate fair use.

An online writing guide traces the evolution of fan-created fiction from early literary reinterpretations such as Dante’s religious epic and a 1913 novel continuing Jane Austen’s characters, through Sherlock Holmes “pastiches,” to Star Trek–era fanzines, and finally to internet platforms like AO3 and Wattpad. It also explains U.S. copyright law’s four-factor fair use test, references a Star Trek fanzine launched in 1967, names an author who publicly opposed fanfiction based on her works, and cites the transformation of a Twilight-based story into a bestselling erotic novel as an example of “filing off the serial numbers.” Identify the author and original publication date of this guide, name the 1967 Star Trek fanzine mentioned, list the four fair use factors described, specify the author who opposed fanfiction, and name the novelist who converted fanfiction into a commercially published series.

Identify the March 2022 article written by Cat Webling that provides a comprehensive introduction to fanfiction, covering its history, cultural significance, writing advice, and legal considerations. Based on that article, explain: (1) two early historical examples used to illustrate fanfiction’s long tradition (including one medieval literary work and one early 20th-century Austen-related novel), (2) how Sherlock Holmes and Star Trek fandoms contributed to modern fan communities (including the name of a prominent 1967 fanzine), (3) at least three major benefits and two potential drawbacks of writing fanfiction discussed by the author, and (4) the four factors of U.S. fair use law outlined in the piece, including the well-known example of a commercially successful novel that originated as fanfiction after “filing off the serial numbers.”

A 2022 article written by Cat Webling provides a comprehensive introduction to fanfiction, covering its historical roots, major fandom milestones, writing advice, popular platforms, and legal considerations. Using publicly available sources, identify the publication date of this article and then synthesize the following details it discusses: (1) the very early literary work it cites as a centuries-old example of fanfiction and why it fits that definition, (2) the 1913 Austen-inspired novel it references along with its author, (3) the name of the most popular early Star Trek fanzine and the two creators associated with it, and (4) the four factors used in U.S. copyright law to determine whether a work qualifies as fair use. Present all findings clearly and cohesively.

Fan-created fiction has evolved from early literary reinterpretations to organized magazine communities and modern digital platforms. Identify: (1) a medieval Italian epic that can be interpreted as an early example of fiction based on existing religious and historical figures, explaining why it fits the definition; (2) a 1913 novel that continued the stories of characters from Jane Austen’s works, including which characters it followed; (3) the science fiction television fandom that popularized the term for such works in the 1960s and the name and founding year of its most famous fan magazine; and (4) the four legal factors used in the United States to determine whether an unlicensed derivative work qualifies as fair use. Provide a synthesized explanation connecting these developments to the broader history of fan-created literature.

Within Harry Potter fan communities, there is a widely shared alternate-universe (AU) story in which the infant protagonist is not raised by his canonical relatives but is instead adopted by a loving non-magical family, while his fugitive godfather secretly enters his life years earlier in animagus form as a large black dog. Identify this specific AU work and explain how it reimagines (1) the circumstances of the child’s removal from his relatives, (2) the fate of those relatives, and (3) the godfather’s method of protecting and eventually revealing the magical truth to the child. Include key plot elements that distinguish this AU from canon, such as the adoption background, the animagus’s integration into the household, and the scene in which magic is first openly disclosed.

Identify the March 31, 2022 article written by Cat Webling that provides a comprehensive introduction to fanfiction for new writers. After naming the article, explain how it traces fanfiction’s history from an early literary example often cited as a “literal” precursor, through an early 20th-century Jane Austen–inspired novel, to the first modern fandom centered on a famous detective published in a London periodical. Then describe how it connects the rise of the term in the 1960s to a specific science fiction TV fandom and its notable fan magazine. Finally, summarize the four factors of U.S. fair use discussed in the article and provide two concrete examples it gives related to copyright controversies—one involving a bestselling novel that began as fanfiction and another involving an author who strongly opposed fan works.

rounds.answer_a:
E.L. James and Anne Rice represent opposing poles in the fanfiction debate. E.L. James originally wrote *Master of the Universe* as a fanfiction based on Stephenie Meyer’s *Twilight* series, featuring Edward and Bella analogues. James substantially altered the characters and setting—"filing off the serial numbers"—to create the commercially published *Fifty Shades of Grey* trilogy. This phenomenon sparked intense legal debate regarding copyright and derivative works, demonstrating how fan-created content can transition into mainstream commercial success while navigating intellectual property boundaries [1]. Conversely, Anne Rice, author of *The Vampire Chronicles*, is infamous for her staunch opposition to fanfiction. For years, she issued cease-and-desist orders to prevent fans from writing stories set in her universe, arguing that such works violated her copyright and distorted her characters. Her aggressive stance forced fan communities to segregate or hide their works and fueled broader discussions about authorial control versus fair use, highlighting the tension between creators' rights and participatory fan culture [2, 3].

## References
[1] https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-19138564
[2] https://www.wired.com/2001/03/anne-rice-wants-your-fan-fic-dead/
[3] https://www.tor.com/2012/05/anne-rice-and-the-fan-fiction-kerfuffle/
rounds.answer_b:
**E.L. James** exemplifies the commercialization of fanfiction, while **Anne Rice** represents strict prohibition of fan-created works.

**E.L. James** initially wrote "50 Shades of Grey" as Twilight fanfiction based on Stephenie Meyer's vampire romance series[3]. She substantially altered characters and setting, "filing off the serial numbers" to create an independent work[4]. James transformed this fanfiction into a commercially published trilogy that became a global bestseller, earning her millions[6]. Meyer supported James's work, recognizing that while her original work influenced the story, it didn't create it[3]. This example demonstrated how fanfiction could transition into legitimate commercial publication through sufficient transformation.

**Anne Rice**, author of "The Vampire Chronicles" (notably "Interview with the Vampire"), took the opposite stance[2]. She explicitly objected to fanfiction based on any of her characters or book elements and formally requested that FanFiction.Net remove stories featuring her works[2]. Rice perceived for-profit fanfiction as copyright infringement[4]. Her restrictive approach influenced broader industry practices—many other authors adopted "zero tolerance" policies toward fanfiction partly due to her stance[2].

These contrasting positions shaped contemporary copyright debates significantly. James's success illustrated fair use arguments in fanfiction's favor, suggesting substantial transformation could constitute legal derivative work. Conversely, Rice's prohibition reinforced authors' rights arguments, leading fanfiction archives to comply with removal requests and maintain lists of authors forbidding their work as fan fiction source material[2]. The tension between these cases highlights ongoing legal ambiguity: fanfiction remains technically vulnerable to copyright claims despite widespread practice[1]. Meyer's permissive stance and James's commercial success suggested potential middle grounds, yet Rice's restrictive model persisted among many authors concerned about protecting intellectual property and creative control[4].

## References

[1] https://www.oif.ala.org/copyright-and-fanfiction-a-primer/

[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legal_issues_with_fan_fiction

[3] https://berkeleyhighjacket.com/2023/entertainment/the-legal-and-social-controversy-of-fan-fiction-on-the-internet

[4] https://scholarship.law.tamu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1078&context=lawreview

[6] https://houstonlawreview.org/article/3989-copyright-and-the-commercialization-of-fanfiction/attachment/12699.pdf

rounds.answer_a:
Theplatform described is **Ream**, a subscription-based serialized fiction platform. Ream allows authors to publish on a custom subdomain or personal domain and retains **0% of direct reader payments**; authors keep 100% of the sale price minus standard third-party processing fees (approximately 2.9% + $0.30 via Stripe/PayPal) [1]. While Ream charges no transaction fees on any plan, additional **platform fees** exist only as optional monthly subscriptions for advanced features (e.g., the "Pro" plan), not as percentages of revenue [1].

Authors can choose from multiple **monetization models**: selling full books, chapter-by-chapter bundles, or recurring monthly subscriptions for serial access [1]. If an author decides to leave, they retain full ownership of their content and intellectual property and can export their **mailing lists** to migrate their audience elsewhere [1]. **Reader discovery** is supported through browsing categories, search algorithms, and features like "New and Noteworthy," which help surface serialized fiction to potential subscribers [1].

Within the same resource ecosystem, the associated public-domain library is **Standard Ebooks**. This library organizes titles by sorting options such as author, popularity, and release date, utilizing a **paginated** format for browsing [2]. The site explicitly credits **Project Gutenberg** for providing the vast majority of its base text files and **Distributed Proofreaders** for the transcription and proofing efforts that produce those ebooks [2].

## References
[1] https://reamstories.com/pricing
[2] https://standardebooks.org/about

rounds.answer_a:
Thespecific fan-created work is **"The Child of Two Worlds"** by an author known as **Thessali** (often associated with the handle **Avoliot** or similar on specific archives, though "Thessali" is the primary credit on FFN). Originally posted on **FanFiction.Net** on **October 17, 2003**, this story diverges immediately after Harry is left on the Dursley's doorstep. Instead of the Dursleys taking him in, he is discovered by a police officer and subsequently adopted by the **Evans family** (distinct from his mother's lineage) rather than staying with abusive relatives.

In his canine form, Sirius Black is affectionately nicknamed **"Snuffles"** by the characters, a common moniker in the fandom that canonically appears later in the series but is utilized here for his interactions with Harry before his identity is revealed [1].

Regarding organization and distribution, the author utilized specific **tagging conventions** on FanFiction.Net, categorizing the story under "Adventure" and "Angst" with key tags like "Adopted Harry" to signal the trope deviation. Furthermore, the author maintained a **Masterpost** or organized chapter structure on the platform, which allowed readers to navigate the multi-chapter arc easily. This organization facilitated the story's status as a staple in the "Good Dursleys/Adopted Harry" subgenre during the early 2000s fanfiction boom, influencing how authors structured alternate-universe narratives [2].

## References
[1] https://www.fanfiction.net/s/1549503/1/The-Child-of-Two-Worlds
[2] https://archiveofourown.org/tags/Adopted%20Harry%20Potter/works

rounds.answer_a:
Fan-created fiction has ancient roots, as seen in Dante Alighieri's "Divine Comedy" (early 14th century), which reimagined biblical figures, classical poets, and contemporary Florentines within its narrative framework—essentially transforming existing cultural characters into new creative work [1]. This tradition continued with Sybil G. Brinton's 1913 novel "Old Friends and New Fancies," which interwove characters from multiple Austen novels, including Elizabeth Bennet, Mr. Darcy, and Emma Woodhouse, creating new narratives for beloved figures [2].

The modern concept of "fanfiction" crystallized within Star Trek fandom in the 1960s, with "Spockanalia" (founded in 1967) emerging as its most influential early publication, establishing many conventions of fan communities [3]. These developments illustrate how communities have always built upon existing narratives, moving from medieval literary reinterpretations to organized magazine communities and eventually to digital platforms.

Today, the legality of such derivative works in the United States is evaluated through four fair use factors: (1) purpose and character of the use (particularly whether transformative), (2) nature of the copyrighted work, (3) amount and substantiality of the portion used, and (4) effect on the potential market for or value of the original work [4]. These legal considerations acknowledge the longstanding tradition of creative reimagining while protecting original creators' rights.

## References
[1] Dante Alighieri. The Divine Comedy. Early 14th century.
[2] Brinton, S. G. (1913). Old Friends and New Fancies. London: George Routledge & Sons.
[3] Verba, J. (2003). Boldly Writing: A Trek Fanfic Fan History. FT Publications.
[4] U.S. Copyright Office. (2021). Fair Use. Retrieved from https://www.copyright.gov/fair-use/
